title: Correlation Search Performance
spl:
index=_internal sourcetype=scheduler savedsearch_name="*Correlation*"
| stats avg(run_time) as avg_runtime, max(run_time) as max_runtime by savedsearch_name
| where avg_runtime > 60
| sort -avg_runtime
description: Slow or resource-intensive correlation searches degrade ES performance and may miss detections if they timeout.
value: Slow or resource-intensive correlation searches degrade ES performance and may miss detections if they timeout.
implementation: Monitor ES correlation search run times from `_internal`. Alert when searches exceed their schedule interval (running longer than they should). Identify skipped searches. Optimize SPL for slow searches.
visualization: Table (search performance), Bar chart (avg runtime by search), Line chart (runtime trend), Single value (skipped searches).
knownFalsePositives: One-off long runs when enabling many rules, rebuilding summaries, or during data backfills; compare to search load and content pushes.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk internal metrics.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `_internal` scheduler logs.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Monitor ES correlation search run times from `_internal`. Alert when searches exceed their schedule interval (running longer than they should). Identify skipped searches. Optimize SPL for slow searches.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=_internal sourcetype=scheduler savedsearch_name="*Correlation*"
| stats avg(run_time) as avg_runtime, max(run_time) as max_runtime by savedsearch_name
| where avg_runtime > 60
| sort -avg_runtime
```

Understanding this SPL

**Correlation Search Performance** — Slow or resource-intensive correlation searches degrade ES performance and may miss detections if they timeout.

Documented **Data sources**: `_internal` scheduler logs. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk internal metrics. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: _internal; **sourcetype**: scheduler. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=_internal, sourcetype=scheduler. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by savedsearch_name** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Filters the current rows with `where avg_runtime > 60` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 — Validate
Compare with the source-of-truth console (cloud CSP, EDR, or IdP) when the data allows it. Check the Splunk Security Essentials catalog for the same detection name, and review the ES Incident Review queue for related notables. Verify key fields, time windows, and index access for your search role.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (search performance), Bar chart (avg runtime by search), Line chart (runtime trend), Single value (skipped searches).
grandmaExplanation: We use this 'Correlation Search Performance' search to catch the exact behavior in the data we already collect, so the team can confirm or clear it in the right management console or identity store.
